sentences.text: Yeah, OK.
So I met Liliana.
And I-- we decided to go to-- to partisan, to-- to Soviet partisan in the east part of Poland, occupied by Germany.
And I volunteered to-- not volunteered, but I-- but I took an appointment or employment with this Polish company.
And they sent me and Liliana together because I presented her as my wife.
And I said that the conditions to go to this town, Dubno, is that I will go with my wife.
They agreed, and they sent us to-- it was a-- a kind of transport because there was more people involved.
Some workers were also going there and so on.
And we traveled to that place, Dubno.
And I started to-- to be the manager of that group.
My [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] duties were to-- to have those people registered.
And because those Polish people, the-- the workers, they were living in-- in houses which were given to-- to our disposal.
And they were eating there, and there was a girl who was a cook for this group.
And I had to-- it was ordinary, you know, managerial duties.
And I had to record how many hours they were doing and so on and be also a liaison between a few German soldiers.
Usually, there were the non-commissioned officers.
And-- and they had a small office in the city.
And they were giving the orders, what those group has to do.
And I had to transfer these-- these orders to those Polish people.
And so it was during the summer of 1943.
And because we were sleeping in one room which was adjacent to the other rooms where those workers were sleeping, and well, Polish people, they like vodka.
And sometimes, they get drunk and so on.
And we could hear through the walls what they sometimes, under, you know, the influence of vodka were talking or saying or screaming and so and so.
So there was definite [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] instances that we heard that they were spreading on me and calling me, oh, special-- using special words.
And I recognized right away they are assuming that I'm a Jew.
Not my wife Lilian, but me, personally me.
So after I think a month of being there, we felt to be endangered.
So that's-- the situation sometimes will be very-- very critical.
So we had-- we agreed-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Lilka and me-- Lilian and me, we agreed that we have to get out of this place.
It's impossible to stay here.
It's too dangerous.
So during one of visit to-- to this office, of this German soldier's office, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] actually, that's-- she was that brave person-- did something which saved us later.
Apparently, there was-- all the time, there was a traffic.
And one of my duties was to-- to report to those Germans that somebody has to go back to Warsaw.
And for that reason, he had to have a-- a-- a document, of-- travel document.
And those travel documents were given by those Germans.
Only with this travel document, you could travel.
Apparently, those Polish workers, they didn't come to that place in order to-- to work for Germans or something like that.
It was precedence.
Because they were coming to this place.
This region was cultivating tobacco.
And they were working for a few days or weeks and then finding some reason to go back to Warsaw.
And at the same time, they were transporting loads of tobacco.
And that was the black market of tobacco.
Because in Poland, the cigarettes-- in Warsaw, the cigarettes were very expensive.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I knew how this procedure was going.
And in this office, there was an older soldier.
It was a surgeon.
And he was such a-- a military bureaucrat.
He was keeping those documents in his desk and so on.

And every time when I-- when I need a document for the-- for one of these Polish-- Polish guys going back to Warsaw, I had to go to his office and ask and explain and so on that I was given such a document.
So sometimes, Liliana was also going with me, just for-- for relaxation and for curiosity.
And they were-- those particular-- this particular person was quite-- quite friendly.
And of course, he didn't suspect anything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And one day, when we already decided that we have to get out of this city, we came to this soldier or to this German soldier.
And we were talking in different aspect and so on and so on.
And somehow, we managed that he had to go to the other room.
There were several rooms.
But he was sitting at the desk in-- in one room, in the first room.
And when he went to the other room, Lilian sneaked to his desk.
And-- because she knew where those documents-- those travel documents were lying.
And she took one travel document-- document.
We just stole the document.
The documents were already signed, but only the name was not filled in.
And she took it.
And then we left.
And there's-- there's the-- apparently, the next time when we had to go, he was complaining that he'd counted those documents.
There's one document that's missing, and he doesn't remember whom he gave it.
And he asked me if I got such a document.
I said, no, I don't-- I don't ask any document.
So he knew that one document was missing.
And this document we kept with us.
Because when we-- there was opportunity to do something, to get out of Dubno.
Apparently, I got the message that the management from Warsaw is coming for inspection-- how we are doing here, how we are working for the Germans, and so on and so on.
They were paid for it.
And I heard Saturday from this Polish company.
And so definitely, there was the case.
And they came for the weekend from Warsaw.
And of course, they came to-- to see us, me and-- and Lilian.
And we were talking, and we gave them impression that Lilian is pregnant.
And she has-- because of course, we don't want to have a child-- she has to go to Warsaw to make abortion.
Apparently, this trick worked, and they believed.
And I asked them to take Lilian with them.
And of course, because they have the papers, so they-- they could take Lilian and go to Warsaw.
So they did that.
But the next day, I-- we decided that I will go using this stolen [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] traveling document, that I will do by my-- myself, alone, next day also to Warsaw.
And we will meet in Warsaw.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Interesting at that time that those people, the Polish people, they were suspecting something.
And all the day-- when Lilian left, the day before it was Saturday, she left with those people from Warsaw, management people.
They suspected that if Lilian went, so maybe I will do it also, that I will also leave this place.
So they were spying on me.
So for example, the next day, it was Sunday.
This one guy came and say, do you-- do you go to this church today?
Because it's Sunday.
And I said, oh, of course I'm going to the church.
So I know that he was with me.
And he was in church, and he was watching me, what I'm doing.
Meantime, I made a-- a contact with one nice Polish boy who was there.
He was not a-- a common worker or something.
He was intelligent, and he-- he was, at that time, educated already.
He-- he was a student of-- of high school, but not during the occupation.
But anyway, he was intelligent guy.
And I told him that I'm planning to-- to run away from this place.
And apparently, he was very unhappy there because he was living with this ordinary, common people and didn't fit to it.
And he was unhappy.
And he want to-- want also to-- to go.
Apparently, I knew that this document can be filled such a way that the-- there will be filled one name plus one.
You know, the-- the-- the Germans and the military-- not only German military, but all military is doing this way.
So they are sending one man with the name written on the document plus one.
And who is the one?
Doesn't matter.
So I filled this document with my name, and I add plus one.
And that was the young boy.
So I went with this young boy.
Oh, he was maybe two or three years younger than me, but I call him young boy.
So we went first to this church.
And-- and of course, we knew-- I have seen those guys watching me.
They were also in the church, in church, and so on.
And after the church, I took him.
And we went to-- to the river, to the shore of the river, where one-- where the boats to-- to rent.
And we rented a boat, and we went for-- you know, a ride on-- on the boat.
Because in this town, there was quite a large river.
And after that-- so when we went to the boat, this-- this guy who was watching us in church, he could not go because he-- he was doing it secretly.
So he went back home.
So we went on the boat.
And after an hour-- because we know that at 3 o'clock, there will be freight train going through the city.
And usually, you know, in the eastern of Poland, all these [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] train tracks were outside of the cities.
That was the policy of the Russian government because this region was under Russian-- under Russian before World War I.
So the-- the-- the railroad was quite a distance from the city.
It was not in the city.
And we know that at 3 o'clock, there is always every day a-- a freight train going toward Warsaw-- toward Lemberg, toward Lublin-- Lwow, and Warsaw.
So after this rowing the boat, we left the-- the boat at the-- you know, back.
And-- and through-- through the fields, we went to-- close to the railroad, and we waited in bushes until we hear the rail-- the trains coming.
And apparently, this place, there was a hill.
So the freight trains, which are quite long, they were empty because they were going to-- to pick up the ammunition and all-- all the things that they need on the-- the front.
The front was quite far away from that place in Russian.
So we-- we-- we just listened for the-- for the sounds of the train, and definitely the train came.
And because of the hill, the train had to slow down.
When the train slowed down, so we jumped on-- on one of the train carriages.
But of course, there were the freight train.
So we were-- he, this guy and me, on separate trains or separate carriages.
And this way, we came to-- to Lwow, to Lemberg.
And-- and there, we-- we went out, outside, and where the civilians were.
And-- and I-- we were going just straight to the-- we-- we-- we thought that there will be a train to Warsaw.
And we thought that my-- my Lilian already is in Warsaw because they departed the day before.
And when we're going on-- on the platform just openly like that, suddenly I noticed there was a-- a train standing there.
And suddenly, I notice that somebody is waving from a-- from a window of the train.
And I-- coming closer, I notice that was my Lilian.
So apparently, there was something on-- on the tracks of the-- of-- of-- for that train, that the train didn't depart yesterday while-- how it should, but is departing the next-- after.
The next day after.
So she gave me-- of course, she was not-- not alone.
She was in company with those people from Warsaw.
So she-- I understood right away that I should not come and see her.
So we boarded the train, but the last-- last-- last carriage.
And this way, we came to Warsaw.
We came to Warsaw.
And of course, when we came to Warsaw, right away, we went to my previous apartment where I lived on Padewska.
And when I came there, the-- the-- the landlady opened the door.
And she gave me-- because Lilian was not there.
She was on the street.
On the street.
So she gave bad news.
She told me that she cannot let me in.
Apparently, in this apartment, my sister was living now.
She took my apartment, and she was living there.
But this landlady told me that she cannot let me in because the police is looking after me.
And-- and also, she took me there are some rumors that I met some [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Soviet Union agent, a spy, and so on-- you know, such wild rumors.
But she-- she's afraid to let me in.
OK, so I said, OK.
So I took Lilian, and we went on the main station in Warsaw, railroad station.
And we spent the whole night over there in the main railroad station.
Next day, we decided to go the other day.
And this way was-- I knew already.
I had the [INAUDIBLE].
There was another recruiting office in Warsaw.
And they were recruiting people to Kapfenberg in Austria.
The Kapfenberg is a-- a famous [SIGHS] steel works in Austria in Steiermark, province of Steiermark.
It was 300 kilometers beyond Vienna.
And they were looking for people.
And because I had some kind of-- of a mechanical background, so I thought maybe they will accept me, and they will take me.
Take me.

So at that time, of course, the German industry were eager to find workers, and they didn't want to-- to-- well, they preferred, of course, somebody who is-- who is volunteering to work for them than-- than-- than-- the-- the other, you know, forced people.
So I went to this office.
And-- and apparently, they-- shortly speaking, they accepted me and Lilian to go to the Kapfenberg, to that-- to this place, to this Steiermark.
And there was some procedure, and we had to go to a certain location in Warsaw where they were the-- performing the medical examination.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- those people had to sleep there.
And until the transport was organized, a group-- When did you arrive in Austria?
So we-- it was, I think, a beginning.
I think, 16-- I don't remember the date.
But it was in September, 1943.
We came to Vienna-- to Vienna, of course.
And in Vienna, we changed the train, and we came to Kapfenberg, Kapfenberg to that city.
And interesting-- in this Kapfenberg, apparently, the recruiting company who was recruiting people to work for this bohlerstahl company were Danish company.
And when we came there, that transport, the two Danish young men appeared, and this was the first time I heard the Danish language.
And I was wondering, that sounds like German, but I could not understand a word of it.
The plant, it was Danish.
And those Danish-- interesting.
We had to go to-- to-- to certain places in this Kapfenberg to-- to receive the certain uniforms and this and those.
And they had to escort us.
Interesting that they were trying to keep a distance between us.
Because you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they-- they were always running ahead of us, but quite a distance.
And we had to really run after them.
You know, I had this impression that they are ashamed of being connected with us.
You know, they were treating us like-- like lepers.
Oh, well, they were collaborating with Germans, and so-- and you know, the occupation of-- of Denmark was quite friendly.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So anyway.
So we start-- well, I was quarreling with this company that I wanted to-- to-- to work as a designer or something like that, or-- but not as ordinary worker.
And they were trying to force me-- and of course, the point was that I didn't want to be separated from my wife, Lilian.
And that was a problem.
Because all the workers, male, were living in one camp, and the-- the female. were living in another camp.
And they wanted to separate us.
And we were opposing.
So anyway, there was a smaller room where the office of that company, of this Danish company, was there with beds.
So it's like-- like a kind of a motel, but for-- just for two people.
So we asked to-- that until they find us the occupation or employment in such a way that we could stay together, and of course, some-- some living quarters-- so we were using this.
So anyway, a few days, we were sleeping in this small room next to this office.
And one-- there was-- I think it was next Sunday or something.
We heard that the new transport of workers from Warsaw is coming to this company, to this Kapfenberg company.
So of course-- because we were sleeping there anyway.
So we went out to meet those people to see.
And we were interested in who is coming.
And when-- when we are standing, it was such-- such a yard.
On this yard.
And there [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a car came into this yard, and a truck came to this yard.
And the people start to jump out of this-- of this truck.
And Lilian was standing next to me, at quite a distance, maybe 500 meters from-- from that truck.
And suddenly, I see that Lilian is walking toward those people who came.
And I-- I stay.
And I notice that she is giving hand and-- to somebody there.
And I couldn't hear what she was talking to him.
But anyway, she was receiving him as a very good acquaintance.
It's that she knew him.
And after this exchange of some-- some talk with him, she comes back to me, and she says those words.
We are lost.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And I ask, why?
This guy, I know him from Warsaw.
Now-- now we-- she-- she was telling me-- me how it come out.
Apparently, when before she [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] acquainted me, or before meeting me, she was renting a room at one family.
And those were mother and daughter.
And the daughter was a wife of a Polish officer who was in-- not there, of course.
Because all Polish officers were interned in some camps.
And she was living in this apartment, villa, with her mother.
Interesting that in order that Lilian is not understanding what they are talking, they were talking between them in French.
French.
And Lilian didn't tell that she speaks French.
Because Lilian, in high school, she was taking French.
So she could hear what they were talking behind her back.
So anyway, this is not the point right now.
But apparently, they were to-- to this house, to this family, there was a cleaning lady coming.
And she was cleaning those two ladies' rooms.
And also, same time, she was cleaning the room where Lilian was living.
And of course, she was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] telling those landladies that she is a daughter of wealthy farmer, Polish farmer.
And she was afraid to be grabbed for the forced labor in Germany.
So she run away from that place where her parents are living, and she came to Warsaw, and she lives in this room.
So you understand the environment which was given.
OK, we'll talk more in a minute.
Mm-hm.
